passage: Psychologists studying memory retention conducted an experiment comparing three study techniques over a four-week period. They argue that spaced repetition produces superior long-term retention compared to both massed practice and passive review, as measured by the percentage of material recalled after four weeks.
stem: Which choice most effectively uses data from the figure to support the psychologists' argument?

choices.label: A
choices.text: After four weeks, participants using spaced repetition recalled 74% of the material, compared to 45% for massed practice and 31% for passive review, making spaced repetition the most effective technique at every time point.

choices.label: B
choices.text: At the one-week mark, massed practice participants recalled 68% while spaced repetition participants recalled 72%, a difference of only 4 percentage points.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Passive review participants showed the steepest decline, dropping from 65% recall at one day to 31% at four weeks, a loss of 34 percentage points.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Spaced repetition participants started at 78% recall after one day and declined to 74% after four weeks, showing only a 4-point decline compared to larger drops for the other methods.

explanation: The claim is that spaced repetition produces superior four-week retention compared to both alternatives. Choice A directly compares all three techniques at the four-week mark (74% vs. 45% vs. 31%), clearly showing spaced repetition's superiority. Choice B focuses on one-week results where the gap is small. Choice C discusses passive review's decline without comparing to spaced repetition's four-week outcome. Choice D discusses spaced repetition's own trajectory without comparing the four-week endpoint to the other methods.

distractorRationale.A: Correct: compares all three methods at four weeks, showing spaced repetition at 74% is substantially higher than massed practice (45%) and passive review (31%).
distractorRationale.B: Wrong (misread value): cites one-week data where the gap between spaced repetition and massed practice is small, which actually weakens the impression of spaced repetition's superiority.
distractorRationale.C: Wrong (irrelevant data): discusses passive review's decline trajectory without directly comparing four-week outcomes across all methods.
distractorRationale.D: Wrong (incomplete evidence): shows spaced repetition's own stability but omits the four-week comparison to both alternatives needed to support the claim.

imageDescription: Line graph titled 'Material Recalled Over Time by Study Technique.' X-axis: Time After Study (1 Day, 1 Week, 2 Weeks, 4 Weeks). Y-axis: Percentage Recalled (0-100%). Three lines: Spaced Repetition: 78%, 72%, 76%, 74%. Massed Practice: 75%, 68%, 55%, 45%. Passive Review: 65%, 52%, 40%, 31%. Source: Cognitive Psychology Research Lab, 2024.

chartData.title: Material Recalled Over Time by Study Technique

passageMetadata.source: Adapted from Cognitive Psychology Research Lab experimental study
